items.title: Friedreich's ataxia: GAA mutation
items.contentHtml:
Friedreich's ataxia and GAA mutation

  • Friedreich's ataxia is the most common hereditary ataxia; autosomal recessive inheritance due to GAA triplet expansion in the FXN (frataxin) gene.
  • Mitochondrial frataxin regulates iron metabolism; its deficiency causes oxidative stress and neurodegeneration.
  • Clinic: spinocerebellar ataxia, dysarthria, loss of vibratory and proprioceptive sensitivity, Babinski sign, scoliosis, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
  • Diagnosis: genetic study that detects >66 GAA repeats (normal <30).
  • Trap: do not confuse with ataxia due to vitamin E deficiency; Friedreich's ataxia presents normal levels of vitamin E and no response to supplementation.

items.title: Postvaricella acute cerebellar ataxia
items.contentHtml:
Post-varicella acute cerebellar ataxia

  • Most common neurological complication of chickenpox in children, typically 1-3 weeks after the rash.
  • Pathophysiology: post-infectious immune-mediated mechanism, not direct viral invasion of the cerebellum.
  • Clinical: acute gait ataxia, dysmetria, intention tremor, nystagmus; benign and self-limited course in weeks.
  • Differential diagnosis: Guillain-Barré syndrome ataxic variant (ophthalmoplegia, areflexia); Cerebellar encephalitis due to varicella-zoster (fever, altered consciousness, cerebrospinal fluid pleocytosis).
  • Treatment: symptomatic; Acyclovir is not required except in immunocompromised patients.

items.title: Classification of hereditary ataxias
items.contentHtml:
Classification of hereditary ataxias

  • They are divided into autosomal recessive (e.g. Friedreich's ataxia, ataxia telangiectasia, ataxia due to vitamin E deficiency) and autosomal dominant (spinocerebellar ataxias, SCA).
  • The spinocerebellar ataxias (SCA)are classified by the mutated gene; the most common is SCA3 (Machado-Joseph disease), due to expansion of CAG triplets in the ATXN3 gene.
  • Episodic ataxia: type 1 (potassium channelopathy) with myocymia; type 2 (calcium channelopathy) with vertiginous nystagmus, responds to acetazolamide.
  • X-linked ataxias include Kennedy disease (spinobulbar muscular atrophy).

items.title: Ataxia telangiectasia and AFP
items.contentHtml:
Ataxia telangiectasia and elevated AFP

  • Ataxia telangiectasia: autosomal recessive disease due to mutation of the ATM gene (ataxia telangiectasia mutated), which encodes a DNA repair kinase.
  • Clinical: progressive cerebellar ataxia since childhood, ocular and skin telangiectasias, immunodeficiency (IgA and IgE deficiency), predisposition to neoplasms (lymphoma, leukemia).
  • Elevated serum alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is a key diagnostic marker; appears from an early age.
  • Diagnosis: AFP levels >2 standard deviations for age, genetic confirmation with ATM sequencing.
  • Trap: elevated AFP is also seen in ataxia due to vitamin E deficiency (AVED), but this does not present telangiectasias or immunodeficiency.

items.title: Romberg sign in sensory ataxia
items.contentHtml:
Romberg sign in sensory ataxia

  • Positive Romberg sign: patient remains stable with eyes open but sways or falls when closing them, indicating loss of proprioception.
  • In sensory ataxia (due to peripheral neuropathy, tabes dorsalis, subacute combined degeneration) the gait is heeling and the Romberg is positive.
  • In cerebellar ataxia the Romberg is negative or false positive (instability both with eyes open and closed, without clear worsening when closing them).
  • Trap: the Romberg can be simulated in patients with vertigo or fear of falling; The test should be performed with feet together and arms close to the body.

items.foco: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis and mixed signs
items.title: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis: mixed signs
items.contentHtml:
Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis and mixed signs

  • Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a degenerative disease of the upper and lower motor neuron, with mixed signs in the same body region.
  • Upper motor neuron signs: hyperreflexia, clonus, spasticity, Babinski sign.
  • Lower motor neuron signs: fasciculations, muscle atrophy, hyporeflexia, flaccid weakness.
  • The simultaneous combination in the same segment (e.g. hand with atrophy and hyperreflexia) is highly suggestive of ALS.
  • Trap: the absence of sensory, oculomotor or sphincter alterations is typical; If they appear, reconsider diagnosis.

items.title: Spinal muscular atrophy type I
items.contentHtml:
Spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) type I

  • Spinal muscular atrophy type I (Werdnig-Hoffmann disease): autosomal recessive due to deletion of the SMN1 gene (survival of motor neuron 1).
  • Onset before 6 months of age; Proximal symmetrical weakness, severe hypotonia, areflexia, lingual fasciculations.
  • Inability to sit without support; death due to respiratory failure before 2 years without treatment.
  • Diagnosis: genetic study with homozygous deletion of SMN1; SMN2 copy number modulates severity.
  • First-line treatment: nusinersen (intrathecal injection) or onasemnogene abeparvovec (intravenous gene therapy).

items.title: Progressive bulbar palsy and dysphagia
items.contentHtml:
Progressive bulbar palsy and dysphagia

  • Progressive bulbar palsy is a variant of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis that predominantly affects the bulbar lower motor neuron.
  • Clinic: dysarthria, dysphagia, lingual atrophy and fasciculations, weakness of facial and pharyngeal muscles; Nausea areflexia.
  • Dysphagia is an early symptom with a poor prognosis, with a risk of bronchoaspiration and malnutrition.
  • Differential diagnosis: myasthenia gravis (fluctuation, fatigue, response to edrophonium), Kennedy's disease (gynecomastia, androgen sensitivity), cerebrovascular accident bulbar (sudden onset).
  • Management: percutaneous gastrostomy when dysphagia compromises caloric intake; speech therapy evaluation.

items.title: El Escorial Criteria for ALS
items.contentHtml:
El Escorial Criteria for ALS

  • The El Escorial Criteria (reviewed in Awaji) classify the diagnosis of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis into: definitive, probable, probable with laboratory support, possible.
  • They require evidence of upper and lower motor neuron involvement in at least one region (bulbar, cervical, thoracic, lumbar) for definitive diagnosis.
  • The Awaji scale incorporates electromyographic findings (fasciculations, positive sharp waves) as evidence of lower motor neuron, comparable to clinical findings.
  • Progression of the disease and exclusion of other causes (neuropathies, myopathies, diseases of the neuromuscular junction) are requirements.
  • Trap: the diagnosis of ALS is clinical; The criteria are research guides, they do not replace clinical judgment.

items.foco: Ataxia due to vitamin E deficiency
items.title: Ataxia due to vitamin E deficiency
items.contentHtml:
Ataxia due to vitamin E deficiency

  • Ataxia due to vitamin E deficiency (AVED): autosomal recessive due to mutation in the APTT gene (alpha-tocopherol transfer protein).
  • Clinic symptoms similar to Friedreich's ataxia (ataxia, areflexia, Babinski sign, sensory neuropathy) but without cardiomyopathy nor severe scoliosis.
  • Diagnosis: serum levels of vitamin E <5 mg/L (normal >5.5 mg/L); genetic confirmation in APTT.
  • Treatment: oral supplementation with alpha-tocopherol 800-1200 mg/day; It can stop or partially reverse the progression if it starts early.
  • Trap: abetalipoproteinemia (Bassen-Kornzweig disease) also causes vitamin E deficiency, but it causes acanthocytosis and steatorrhea.

items.title: Kennedy disease and androgen receptor
items.contentHtml:
Kennedy's disease and androgen receptor

  • Kennedy's disease (X-linked spinobulbar muscular atrophy): expansion of CAG triplets in the androgen receptor (AR) gene on the X chromosome.
  • Onset in adulthood (30-50 years); proximal and bulbar weakness and atrophy, fasciculations, areflexia, cramps.
  • Distinctive signs: gynecomastia, testicular atrophy, infertility due to partial androgen insensitivity.
  • Slowly progressive course, life expectancy is not significantly reduced.
  • Diagnosis: genetic study showing >38 CAG repeats in AR (normal <36).
  • Trap: do not confuse with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis; Kennedy disease does not affect the upper motor neuron and presents gynecomastia.

items.title: Subacute combined spinal degeneration
items.contentHtml:
Subacute combined spinal cord degeneration

  • Subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord due to deficiency of vitamin B12 (cobalamin), typically in pernicious anemia, gastrectomy, strict veganism, malabsorption.
  • Physiopathology: demyelination of the posterior (proprioception, vibration) and lateral (corticospinal) cords, causing ataxia sensory and spasticity.
  • Clinical: symmetrical paresthesias in hands and feet, positive Romberg sign, ataxic gait, hyperreflexia with Babinski sign, loss of vibratory sensitivity.
  • Diagnosis: serum levels of vitamin B12 <200 pg/mL, methylmalonic acid and homocysteine elevated.
  • Treatment: cyanocobalamin intramuscular 1000 µg/day for 1 week, then weekly for 1 month, then monthly for life.
